story:
Adele was a little drunk in the bistro’s elegant ladies’ room when she asked, “Patrice, can you keep a secret?” We’re two mid-70s women who still keep it together—fresh lipstick, decent dates, and more wit than most of Shady Ridge Senior Living—so yes, I said my lips were sealed. She made me promise no matter what. I laughed and said even if she’d killed a man. “Good,” she said. “Because I did.”

She meant Mayor Buster Dodd, the famously corrupt blowhard who had just died during a city council meeting. “I made it happen,” she said, perfectly calm, as if she’d ordered dessert. She’d “concentrated” on him having a heart attack while watching the meeting on TV. I didn’t believe her, of course, but I was curious—and we were tipsy enough to enjoy the drama.

It became a game. Over breakfast we joked about who deserved to “go,” sticking to big, distant evildoers. Once, when a waiter stumbled right after Adele playfully pointed a knife and said, “You’re next, Bub,” we laughed it off—but we looked at each other, too. Eventually we settled on a homophobic dictator in another country. My grandson is gay, and the man’s cruelty enraged me. We riffed on poetic justice—“bring him down,” we said—imagining absurd solo air mishaps. We giggled; Adele promised, “Tonight’s the night.”

The next morning the news said he’d fallen down a grand marble staircase. We stared at each other in the breakfast room, rattled. Coincidence, we agreed. Adele swore she wouldn’t “concentrate” on anyone again, just in case.

Months passed. We avoided the topic, stuck to movies and double dates. Then my ex, Dick—who’d dumped Adele for “Call me Honey Bee,” the yoga queen—strode by. We muttered that he was a fool, an idiot. A crash in the garden cut us off. Dick had tried to yank down a trellis after hours and went down with it. He died at the hospital two days later.

We’d barely processed that when his daughter stormed into the lobby, huge and furious, threatening to sue and shut Shady Ridge down. She lifted tiny Mrs. Lee, our director, off her feet. People screamed; canes were raised; phones dialed 911. “What a terrible bully,” Adele whispered, pale. The daughter spun toward the door, shouted, and ran outside. Brakes shrieked. A bus hit a light pole. “She came out of nowhere,” the driver sobbed. The daughter lay crumpled in the street.

That evening we talked for hours. Maybe Adele really did have a power—especially when she was close and angry. We couldn’t tell anyone without getting reassigned to Memory Care, so we made a plan: no more fury, no more petty judgments, no more complaints about staff. We practiced empathy. To our surprise, it worked. We got kinder. We relaxed. People said we looked younger. We made more friends, even with Honey, started a literary club, and joined Chair Yoga.

From a safe distance, we tested our theory. Insulting faraway villains didn’t instantly kill them. When Adele did focus, though, certain notorious tyrants did seem to meet swift, suspicious ends. After a third dictator, the internet whispered about a curse. We’re not endorsing vigilante justice; we know killing is wrong. But if Adele’s strange talent exists, we’ve learned restraint, anger sharpens it, distance doesn’t blunt it much, and the world is complicated. Now we keep our tempers in check, choose compassion locally, and—very carefully—consider the worst of the worst. There’s a Senator we’re watching. Stay tuned.
